questions.scenario:
The Plugin Manager shows this warning before an upgrade:

```text
git 5.5.0 requires ssh-credentials version 350.v2c0c6fea_a_22b_
but you have version 343.v884f71d78167 installed.
ssh-credentials will be upgraded automatically.
```

How should you interpret it?

options.text: This is a minimum-version dependency bump. Jenkins can resolve it by installing the newer `ssh-credentials`, but the changed plugin set still belongs on staging first.
This means `git` and `ssh-credentials` cannot ever be installed together.
This proves the running controller is already broken before the restart.
This can be ignored because transitive dependencies never affect runtime behavior.

questions.explanation: The article calls this kind of warning mostly benign compared with API-removal warnings: Jenkins can pick the higher minimum version. It is still a plugin-set change that should be tested away from production.

questions.scenario:
A second warning appears in the same upgrade:

```text
git-client 6.0.0 drops support for the deprecated
GitClient.fetch(String, RefSpec) method. Plugins that call this method
will fail at runtime.
```

Why is this more dangerous than a normal minimum-version bump?

options.text: The install can succeed and the UI can look healthy, but a plugin that still calls the removed method may fail only when a build reaches that code path.
It means Jenkins will refuse to start until every Pipeline is deleted.
It only affects plugins that are missing from `plugins.txt`, not plugins already installed.
It is dangerous only for controllers that use JCasC.

questions.explanation: The article's failure story is exactly this shape: the controller starts, the UI looks fine, and checkout fails later with `NoSuchMethodError` when an older plugin calls a method removed by `git-client`.

questions.scenario:
A checkout fails after a plugin upgrade with this console fragment:

```text
Caused by: java.lang.NoSuchMethodError:
  org.jenkinsci.plugins.gitclient.GitClient.fetch(...)
  at hudson.plugins.git.GitSCM.fetchFrom(GitSCM.java:1052)
```

Which investigation steps match the article's diagnosis path?

options.text: Search the controller log for `NoSuchMethodError` or the failing class name.
Check Manage Jenkins > System Information > Plugins to identify the installed plugin versions.
Compare the plugin that called the missing method with the plugin that owns the method.
Immediately reload JCasC because dependency conflicts are YAML syntax errors.

questions.explanation: The article separates plugin runtime failures from configuration reloads. Logs and plugin inventory help identify the caller and owner. JCasC reload does not fix an API mismatch between plugin versions.

questions.scenario:
Two plugins depend on `ssh-credentials`:

```text
git 5.4.0 requires ssh-credentials >= 305.v8f4381501156
kubernetes 4296.v20a_3a_3aff5e1 requires ssh-credentials >= 343.v884f71d78167
```

What version can satisfy both minimums?

options.text: `343.v884f71d78167` or newer, because it satisfies both minimum-version requirements.
`305.v8f4381501156` exactly, because it is the first dependency listed.
No version can satisfy two Jenkins plugins at the same time.
Whichever version is already in `$JENKINS_HOME/plugins/`, even if it is lower than both minimums.

questions.explanation: For normal minimum-version dependencies, Jenkins needs a version at least as high as the strictest requirement. The harder failure is when a newer dependency removes an API that another plugin still calls.

questions.scenario:
A staging controller reproduces the article's failure: an old third-party branch-source plugin calls a method removed from the new `git-client`. There is no compatible release of the third-party plugin.

What is the safest production response?

options.text: Block the promotion and either pin `git-client` to the compatible version for now or replace the unmaintained plugin.
Promote anyway because production might not hit that checkout path.
Click Update All again in production so Jenkins can find a better combination automatically.
Remove `plugins.txt` so dependencies resolve dynamically on every controller start.

questions.explanation: The point of staging is to catch exactly this runtime break before users do. If no compatible third-party plugin exists, you either hold the dependency at a compatible version temporarily or replace the stale plugin.
